ground_truth.title: Nagel on Reason
ground_truth.author: David Gordon
ground_truth.main_content: One of the dominant superstitions of our time is that truth is relative and not absolute. As the philosopher Thomas Nagel says in The Last Word (Oxford University Press, 1997), A vulgar version of this skepticism about reason is epidemic in the weaker regions of our culture... Nagel presents a philosophically deep criticism of the relativist view. One of his key points is that challenges to reason contradict themselves because the arguments given in the challenge rely on reason. Nagel refutes the subjectivist claim that 'Everything is subjective' by arguing it must be either subjective or objective, but cannot be objective (since then it would be false if true) and cannot be subjective (because then it would not rule out any objective claim). Nagel challenges Robert Nozick's evolutionary explanation of why we hold certain beliefs, arguing that truth and evolutionary success are different concepts, neither of which entails the other. Reason must always have the last word.
with: One of the dominant superstitions of our time is that truth is relative and not absolute.
In order to have the authority it claims, reason must be a form or category of thought from which there is no appeal beyond itself.
To put it schematically, the claim 'Everything is subjective' must be nonsense, for it would itself be either subjective or objective.
Nagel is saying that 'truth' and 'evolutionary success' are different concepts, neither of which entails the other.
The only form that genuine reasoning can take consists in seeing the validity of the arguments in virtue of what they say.
